top_risk: Authorization/IDOR break: if the API trusts a client-supplied user or guild id, user A reads user B's stats — OAuth scope minimalism cannot prevent this.
cheapest_evidence: Item-by-item attack/defense checklist on a one-endpoint prototype, headlined by an IDOR spike proving identity is derived from the session, never from client input.
report:
## Idea probe — SuperBot Phase-3 personal-stats page (OAuth trust-gate)

(Maps to INTAKE 002; actual SuperBot site/API code not in this repo, so build state/session-store/traffic are not measured.)

### (1) What is this really / the core claim?
Two decoupled things: an architecture decision (interpose a separate superbot-lane read-only token-scoped API serving a versioned data contract between the public site and production Postgres) and a verification gate (owner made trustworthiness a pre-public gate over six named items). Core claim under test: the Phase-3 page is buildable-as-designed AND trustworthy under adversarial verification — a conjunction of ~six security properties plus 'buildable'. Phases 1-2 carved out as no-auth, not gated. That carve-out is the strongest part of the design.

### (2) Strongest failure mode
A conjunction fails if any one item fails. Most dangerous: per-user data isolation / authorization. OAuth identify+guilds establishes who and which guilds — it does nothing to stop an IDOR: stats?user_id=<other>&guild=<x> succeeds if the server reads identity from client input instead of session. Scope minimalism cannot fix an authorization bug. Secondary: scope-minimalism claim may self-undercut (guilds leaks the user's entire server list; membership already known bot-side — whether guilds is needed is not measured). Judgment-only items masquerading as tested: 'no long-lived token storage', 'versioned data contract', 'documented abuse-case walkthrough' have no runnable adversarial dynamics and must be labeled JUDGMENT-ONLY.

### (3) What would falsify it / cheapest adequate evidence
Falsified if any gate item breaks under a concrete attack: IDOR returning another user's stats; missing/unchecked state param permitting login-CSRF; a token persisting past session; a rate limit that never trips. Cheapest evidence: item-by-item attack/defense checklist against a minimal prototype of the OAuth flow + read-only API — demonstrate IDOR blocked (server derives id from session), state round-trip enforced, token exchanged-then-discarded, rate limit trips; review-and-label judgment-only items. Highest-leverage single test is the IDOR/isolation spike.

### (4) Cost / complexity vs payoff
Probe cost: M. Full build cost: higher, partly not measured — a net-new standalone read-only API service in the superbot lane plus site-side OAuth integration and ops. Payoff: user-facing self-service stats; durable value is the isolation boundary keeping prod Postgres unexposed. Phases 1-2 already deliver the data-story value at zero auth cost and are explicitly unblocked.

### (5) Smallest shippable slice
Now: ship Phases 1 and 2 (no auth surface, explicitly not gated). Phase 3 smallest slice: read-only API exposing exactly one endpoint — the logged-in user's own stats for one guild — where the server derives identity solely from the session, behind the login flow with an enforced state parameter. Prove the isolation boundary (IDOR spike) before adding leaderboard/mining breadth, versioned-contract generality, or rate-limit tuning.

### Recommendation rationale
HOLD (on public launch of Phase 3), ship Phases 1-2 in parallel. Design follows known-good OAuth patterns and the gate names the right properties, so not a KILL. But the claim is a security conjunction whose central item (authorization/isolation) is not yet demonstrated, and several items are judgment-only rather than empirically falsifiable — so BUILD would rubber-stamp an unverified gate. Run the cheap adversarial checklist (IDOR-first) to convert HOLD->BUILD. Confidence 0.62: bounded by the undemonstrated isolation item, the possibly-non-minimal guilds scope, and the under-measured net-new-service cost.
